Empires & Puzzles Costumed Ferant Review: Harder, Better, Longer... Weaker?
Costumed Ferant brings modern stats to an aging classic, but at what cost? We break down why -5% mana cut might be a dealbreaker, even with 2000+ Health.
Costumed Ferant Review: The Wolf in Shinier Clothing
Costumed Ferant: The Upgrade That Feels Like a Sidegrade

(Warden of the Wolves) attempts to modernize one of the game's most beloved (and hated) 4-star heroes. By swapping his Druid class for Fighter and giving him a fresh coat of paint, the developers aimed to keep him relevant in the fast-paced meta of 2026. However, the result is a contentious "sidegrade" that trades raw stopping power for sustainability.

The costume transforms Ferant from a wild berserker into a more disciplined, armored knight. He loses the face paint for a cleaner look and dons heavy plate armor with wolf motifs. It’s a cleaner, more "regal" look, possibly implying he has risen in rank within the Wolf order. It’s less "savage" but definitely more "tanky" visually.
The costume provides a much-needed stat overhaul:
A solid jump that brings him closer to modern standards, though still trailing behind the 800+ power of the latest "Construct" or "Astral" 4-stars.

, making him tankier than many older 5-star heroes. This health pool is his greatest asset.
Still on the lower side, but better than the base version's paper-thin 595.
The skill "Fiery Revenge" makes two key adjustments compared to the base version:
A slight increase from 115%. While 5% seems negligible, every bit counts when you are reflecting damage from 2000+ damage snipers.
A -10% cut is often enough to stop a Fast hero from firing after a tile match. A -5% cut might not be. This significantly reduces his ability to "lock down" an enemy team.
The costume is significantly less oppressive as a mana controller.

When released in late 2024, the community reaction was mixed. Players loved the stats but hated the nerf to the mana cut. It was seen as a "safe" costume design—making him tankier but less toxic.
, Costumed Ferant holds up very well stat-wise. He can survive hits from the latest 2026 meta heroes that would vaporize the base version.

Dawn (Minion mana cut) combined with Ferant creates a constant mana drain. The Fighter revive ensures Ferant stays around to protect the squishier heroes.

. While he isn't a Druid anymore, minions still act as a meat shield, preventing him from taking damage and reflecting it. Avoid heavy minion teams if you want max reflection damage.

If you pull the Costume, you have hit the jackpot for 4-star content. Level the Costume first for the stats. Use the Costume version in Rush tournaments for the Revive chance, but don't be afraid to switch to the Base skin if you feel the -5% mana cut isn't doing enough.
The Costume is primarily a stat stick for the Base version in most scenarios. However, do not underestimate the
. In Guild Wars or close tournament matches, that Revive can be the difference between a 0 and a win.
"Lazy costume. Just a stat boost." - Many players were disappointed that the "upgrade" actually nerfed his signature mana cut.
- A common debate. The consensus is that -5% is annoying, but -10% was deadly.
Most veterans agree that swapping Druid (Minions block riposte) for Fighter (Revive) is the best part of the costume.

Costumed Ferant is a paradox: a stronger hero with a weaker skill. He is undeniable proof that stats and class (Fighter > Druid) often matter more than raw skill percentages. He remains a premier 4-star tank in 2026, just slightly less "sharp" than his younger self.
Level for the stats, emblem for the Fighter class, but keep the Base skin handy for when you really need to drain some mana.
A deep dive into Costumed Ferant (Warden of the Wolves). Does the massive stat boost and new passive justify the weaker mana cut? We analyze the trade-offs for 2026.
